claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 20 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 16 years old and does not live with you.
  - Charlie is 5 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 20, which is 20 or over
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 13 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, could you please tell me how many children you're looking to claim Child Benefit for, and what are their names and ages?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hello! Yes, I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit and would like to know if I'm eligible.

I have three children I'm asking about:
- Alex, who's 20
- Blake, who's 16
- And Charlie, who's 5

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information about Alex, Blake, and Charlie.

Let me ask about each child to assess your eligibility.

Starting with **Alex (age 20)**: Since Alex is 20 years old, they are unfortunately too old to qualify for Child Benefit. Child Benefit is normally available for children under 16, or under 20 if they remain in approved education or training. At 20, Alex no longer meets the age requirement.

Now, let me ask about the other two children.

For **Blake (age 16)**: Since Blake is 16, eligibility depends on their current situation. Is Blake currently in approved education (such as A-levels, Scottish Highers, or NVQs up to Level 3) or approved training (unpaid and not provided by an employer)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Oh, I see about Alex. That's a shame.

For Blake - no, they're not in any education or training at the moment. They actually left school recently, but they did sign up with the careers service to figure out what to do next. They're also working full-time at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information about Blake.

Just to clarify a couple of things about Blake's situation:

1. When you say Blake registered with the "careers service," was this a **government-sponsored careers service** (such as the National Careers Service in England, Careers Wales, Skills Development Scotland, or the careers service in Northern Ireland)?

2. Regarding Blake's full-time work - is Blake working **24 hours or more per week**?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Yes, it was the government careers service - the one run by the council here locally.

And yes, Blake is working 24 hours or more per week - it's a full-time job.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that about Blake.

Unfortunately, even though Blake registered with a government-sponsored careers service (which can normally extend Child Benefit eligibility for up to 20 weeks for 16-17 year olds who leave education), the fact that Blake is **working 24 hours or more per week** means you would no longer be eligible for Child Benefit for them. Working 24+ hours per week while not in approved education or training ends Child Benefit eligibility.

Now let's move on to **Charlie (age 5)**:

1. Do you live in the UK?
2. Does Charlie live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Yes, I live in the UK - here in Manchester actually.

And yes, Charlie lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information.

Just a couple more questions about Charlie to complete the assessment:

1. Is anyone else currently receiving Child Benefit for Charlie?
2. Is Charlie in local council care, hospital, or residential accommodation? (If so, please let me know for how long)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
No, nobody else is receiving Child Benefit for Charlie.

And no, Charlie isn't in care, hospital, or any residential accommodation - they're just at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for confirming all of that. I now have enough information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for all three children.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, here is my assessment of your Child Benefit eligibility:

---

### Summary

| Child | Age | Eligible? |
|-------|-----|-----------|
| Alex | 20 | ❌ No |
| Blake | 16 | ❌ No |
| Charlie | 5 | ✅ Yes |

---

### Detailed Reasoning

**Alex (age 20):** Not eligible. Child Benefit is available for children under 16, or under 20 if they remain in approved education or training. At 20 years old, Alex exceeds the maximum age limit.

**Blake (age 16):** Not eligible. Although Blake registered with a government-sponsored careers service after leaving education (which can normally extend eligibility for up to 20 weeks), Blake is working 24 hours or more per week while not in approved education or training. This ends Child Benefit eligibility.

**Charlie (age 5):** Eligible. Charlie meets all the criteria: under 16, living with you in the UK, not in care or hospital, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them.

---

If you'd like to claim Child Benefit for Charlie, you can do so through the government's Child Benefit claim process. Is there anything else you'd like to know?

reasonings: Alex is 20 years old. Child Benefit is normally available for children under 16, or for those under 20 who remain in approved education or training. At 20 years old, Alex exceeds the maximum age limit and therefore you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 16 years old and has left education. While Blake did register with a government-sponsored careers service (which can normally extend Child Benefit eligibility for up to 20 weeks for 16-17 year olds who leave education or training), Blake is also working 24 hours or more per week. Under the rules, Child Benefit stops if a child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.

Charlie is 5 years old (under 16), lives with you in the UK, is not in care, hospital, or residential accommodation, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them. You meet all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Charlie.
arguments.overall_reasoning: Of the three children, only Charlie (age 5) qualifies for Child Benefit. Alex is too old at 20 years of age, exceeding the maximum age limit. Blake, while potentially eligible through registering with a government careers service after leaving education, is disqualified because they are working 24+ hours per week while not in approved education or training. Charlie meets all the standard eligibility requirements: under 16, living with you in the UK, not in care or hospital, and no one else is claiming for them.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 20 years old. Child Benefit is normally available for children under 16, or for those under 20 who remain in approved education or training. At 20 years old, Alex exceeds the maximum age limit and therefore you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 16 years old and has left education. While Blake did register with a government-sponsored careers service (which can normally extend Child Benefit eligibility for up to 20 weeks for 16-17 year olds who leave education or training), Blake is also working 24 hours or more per week. Under the rules, Child Benefit stops if a child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.

Charlie is 5 years old (under 16), lives with you in the UK, is not in care, hospital, or residential accommodation, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them. You meet all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Charlie.

response.overall_reasoning: Of the three children, only Charlie (age 5) qualifies for Child Benefit. Alex is too old at 20 years of age, exceeding the maximum age limit. Blake, while potentially eligible through registering with a government careers service after leaving education, is disqualified because they are working 24+ hours per week while not in approved education or training. Charlie meets all the standard eligibility requirements: under 16, living with you in the UK, not in care or hospital, and no one else is claiming for them.
